data.title: Perrigo: Double-Digit Dividend At A Bargain Price
data.description:
My Investing Philosophy

I like dividend-paying stocks that have a history of raising the dividend consistently, year after year. I also like finding stocks that are currently out of favor, either due to macro events or temporary (but reversible) mistakes by management once they have learned the lesson and are likely to avoid making the same mistake again in the future. I believe today’s subject company, Perrigo (PRGO), fits that definition. With a 12% dividend yield and a P/E of 4.3, it looks like a bargain. So, I did some research to find out why it looks so tempting because many times a company’s yield is high and the P/E is low because it could be going out of business. But this is a stock I believe will rebound eventually, and a patient investor could reap outsized gains while collecting that rising dividend.

I also tend to hold stocks until the company changes its business model (unfavorably, in my opinion), cuts its dividend, or becomes ridiculously overvalued. In each case, I sell and look for a better option. Otherwise, I like to hold stocks forever and let the miracle of compounding and reinvesting dividends do most of the heavy lifting of growing my portfolio and income for me.

Company Background

Perrigo is an OTC (Over-The-Counter) maker of store-brand drugs, infant formula, and animal health products. It operates with two divisions: Consumer Self-Care Americas and Consumer Self-Care International. The company sells thousands of store-branded OTC products, with Walmart accounting for almost 13% of sales.

I like to find companies that have a competitive advantage within their respective industries. Perrigo is a low-cost leader, which enables it to be a valuable partner for discount retailers. Perrigo management tried to move upscale into a higher margin business and learned a difficult lesson. My expectation is that they will stick to what they know best and work on improving margins and volumes within their existing business and network of facilities, only adding small, bolt-on acquisitions that fit within the company’s core competencies.

“Perrigo is a scaled consumer health leader, ranked #1 in terms of volume in the U.S. and a top 10 player by dollar sales across the EU. So sensibly a U.S. and EU business. Our portfolio, as some of you will know, is highly diversified across 9 different categories with no single product contributing more than 5% of total net sales. So, it's a fairly risk-insulated model. We operate in about 30 countries with our commercial footprint, as I mentioned, centered in the U.S. and in Europe.

We deliver meaningful savings for consumers. The majority of our business, about 60% is in store brand offerings.” – Patrick Lockwood-Taylor, President, CEO & Director

The U.S. and EU healthcare systems save about $30B per year derived from generic offerings from Perrigo, according to the CEO in a recent conference presentation. This is a company that will not go under, even though it is priced as though it will.

Its roots go all the way back to 1887, when Luther Perrigo founded the company in Michigan and had the idea to package and distribute medicines and household items to country stores. The company moved its headquarters to Ireland in 2013.

Perrigo’s problems (share price is down 92% over the last ten years) began in 2013 with the acquisition of biotech company Elon for ~$7B, saddling the company with excessive debt. The company divested its prescription drug business in 2025 for $1.5B. The company is now focused on its core business of OTC store-branded products. I think this is good news, as management is now paying attention to improving margins and operational efficiencies rather than growth through acquisitions. I believe this will mark the bottom and a good turnaround point for the company. I also believe the company will be able to remain solvent and continue to raise its dividend, albeit at a much slower pace going forward.

Earnings

I expect that 2026 will be a foundational base year to build off of, given that management guidance forecasts EPS (earnings per share) to be flat to slightly down compared to 2025. Q4 revenue declined by 2%, while adjusted EPS rose by 7% but missed consensus estimates by $0.01. GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) EPS were negative due to a $1.3B non-cash goodwill impairment charge related to the divestitures in 2025.

There is a lot of seasonality to EPS for Perrigo, and when combined with the constant pressure from large retail chains trying to squeeze margins, predicting the future becomes complicated. Looking at the table below, we see a downward trend in both revenue and EPS. But some of that is due to divestitures (especially smaller ones that do not require restatement of financials). Those activities should be minimal in 2026 as management focuses on improving margins through applying operational efficiencies.

Q1 and Q2 of 2026 are expected to be the bottom for Perrigo earnings, with sequential improvements in Q3 & Q4 bringing EPS to a small increase in the final quarter to end the year. I am not sure the Q4 estimate will hold true, but analysts covering the company have EPS coming in at ~$2.11/share, while management is forecasting between $2.25 and $2.55.

Looking out further, I expect EPS to show improvement in 2027 and beyond, but not at a tremendous rate. This is a mature business with limited organic growth prospects except through either cost savings or acquisitions. It appears management is making cost savings their priority.

Growth Prospects

My expectation is for an average of 3% growth in EPS over the next five years, with 2026 forming the base from which to grow. The forward P/E ratio of 4.26 is extremely low, even for a mature company. Since the market is forward-looking, I expect the future earnings growth will begin to have a positive impact on the share price as early as Q2 of this year.

Management expects its Three-S Plan (Stabilize, Streamline, Strengthen) to generate annual cost savings of $80M to $100M per year once fully implemented during 2026. The one-time cost, estimated at $80M to $90M, will weigh on 2026 EPS and margins but is expected to begin to bear fruit in the second half of the year and more fully in 2027 and beyond. Controlling costs is what is core to the company’s culture and strategy. It strayed from that temporarily, learned a valuable lesson, and is now getting back to what it does best.

Profitability

This is a relatively low-margin business, but Perrigo has operated in this environment for most of its existence, and other than the problems that arose from its misguided attempt to stray away from its core, management has consistently generated free cash flow and remained profitable. The profitability grade shown below shows us that Perrigo’s EBIT and EBITDA margins are superior to the sector average, meaning it is not likely to go out of business anytime soon.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet is improving and provides encouragement for investors with a long-term horizon. Management continues to be committed to reducing debt and increasing the dividend and plans to use proceeds from divestitures to reduce debt. The second table below details how much debt has been repaid over the last several years. I like the consistent effort by management.

Other product lines are under review, which could lead to another divestiture or two in 2026 or 2027, and I would expect any such actions to further strengthen the balance sheet.

Dividend

This is the most exciting part for rising income investors like myself. The current yield is 12%, and the payout ratio of 42% leaves ample room for future increases. Increases have averaged 5.2% over the past five years, but I expect the rate going forward will be closer to 3% to match my expectations for overall EPS growth.

My long-term inflation expectation is for an average of 2% to 2.5%, so as long as the dividend increases are able to keep up with that pace, I’ll be happy with my 12.5% yield on my initial investment.

Valuation

I will value the company using two methods. First, I will use the DDM (Dividend Discount Model), and then I will use a method assuming reversion to the mean on a multiple valuation basis. So, the DDM formula looks like this:

(D/(M-X)) = V

Where:

V = Fair Value

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount rate (I use my required rate of return on investment)

X = Expected future average annual increase in dividend

(1.16/(.09-.03)= $19.33

Perrigo is in a slow growth, mature industry with relatively low margins, so my required return is only 9%, the current dividend is $1.16, and the expected growth in the dividend is 3%. When we plug these numbers into the model, the result is $19.33 per share, slightly more than double the current share price of about $9.60. This indicates that the price is a bargain, selling at a discount to fair value of 50.3%. I believe that the price will achieve its fair value within the next five years, so my expected annual compound average price gain will be ~15%, calculated in Excel as follows:

=((19.33/9.60)^(1/5))-1

If we add the 15% annual valuation gain to the dividend yield of 12% and the expected EPS growth rate of 3%, we end up with an expected compound annual average total return of 30.1%, which is probably a stretch. Even if the stock price only achieves half of the valuation gain I project, it would still generate a compound average annual total return of 23%.

Now I want to look at the value from a multiple perspective, assuming that the P/E ratio will revert to its historical mean over the next five years. The 10-year average P/E for PRGO is 15. But with a lower growth rate over the next five years, I expect a more appropriate expectation for the P/E is 9. The current P/E is 4.3, so the stock is trading at a discount of 52.4% of fair value. Fair value is estimated to be $20.16. So, if we calculate the compound average annual increase from a multiple expansion, we get 14.4%. The calculation in Excel is as follows:

=((9/4.3)^(1/5))-1

When we add to the annual expected valuation increase of 14.4%, the current dividend yield of 12%, and the expected EPS growth rate of 3%, we find the expected compound annual total return of 29.4%. That result is very close to the DDM estimate, and either one would be very exciting, especially from a mature OTC products company.

One more way to look at the valuation is to consider how much we would collect from dividends over the next five years plus a projected price based upon a P/E of 9 by the end of the term. If the dividend increases by 3% per year, we can expect to collect ~$6.16 in dividends per share on our initial investment of $9.64. Even if the stock does not appreciate at all, we would have recouped nearly 64% of our initial investment just in dividends. Now, if we assume EPS will grow at 3% per year with forward EPS for 2026 estimated at $2.24/share, EPS in 2030 should be $2.44/share. Multiply that by an expected P/E of 9, and we get an expected share price of $22.03. The gain from $9.64 to $22.03 is $12.43; add the cumulated dividends for a total return of $18.58, or 193% over five years.

Again, it may not achieve the full reversion to the mean I expect, but I can wait a little longer as long as I keep collecting a 12% dividend. My goal is to at least double my initial investment over the next five years. To achieve that, I need the P/E to rise only to 5.36 while EPS grows at only 3%. I really think that is very attainable.

Risks

There are two risks that I foresee. First is that the environment of slow demand for store branded, OTC products continues for many years instead of normalizing. This would be more likely in a booming economy with strong jobs growth. I think we could have decent growth, but with AI (artificial intelligence) eliminating some jobs, it may be presumptuous to expect strong jobs growth over the next five years. A weak employment environment should lead to more consumers opting for the lower cost alternative of OTC, store-branded products over the higher priced name-brand items. So, my assessment of this risk is that the probability is fairly low. But things can change if the war in Iran escalates further and lasts longer than I anticipate.

The second risk is that management forgets its lessons and makes another bad acquisition or that it is unable to generate the expected savings from its Three-S project. Again, I doubt either is likely, but it is possible. No one can see the future, and there could be changes in management personnel bringing with it a new plan.

Either way, I still think the risk is relatively low due to Perrigo’s relations with its customers and its broad product base (over 1,000 products with no one product accounting for more than 5% of revenue).

Conclusion

Perrigo has been beaten down as though it is about to go bankrupt with a P/E of 4.3. The dividend appears safe and, at 12%, is juicy enough for me to buy and hold for the turnaround. I rate Perrigo a Buy for patient investors desiring current income along with eventual appreciation potential.

data.title: Velo3D: Turnaround Goal Just Pushed Further Out, Investors Are Not Happy
data.description:
Introduction

Velo3D, Inc. (VELO) caught my eye because of the massive move after they reported their full-year 2025 results, which showed massive margin deterioration as well as a delay in profitability. I wanted to take a look at the company’s supposed turnaround in more detail and give some comments on why there is still a lot of work to be done.

Briefly on the Company

Velo3D is in the business of making 3D printing systems and related software that allow customers to essentially build custom parts for their needs, as long as the blueprints fit the dimensions of the printer. These printers allow the manufacturing of complex metal parts used in aerospace, defense, and other industrial segments, with the core value proposition being that they let engineers print parts with less compromise and with less support material. Most of the revenue still comes from the sale of its Sapphire printers, but some positive shifts allow for other revenue streams to come through, specifically the Rapid Production solutions, or RPS, which essentially involves parts production, allowing customers to develop applications, and then they can produce the parts in-house or have VELO manufacture them.

By the Numbers

Looking at the headline numbers, it does not look particularly good. Revenue came in at $9.4m, which is down over 25% y/y. We can look at the breakdown in sales in more detail to see that the Support Services segment saw a particularly bad quarter, declining by over 62% to $1.7m from $4.5m. The main revenue generator, which is its 3D printers and parts, also didn’t perform as well as investors would have liked. It is down around 5% y/y to around $7.6m. Now, the following part is where it gets even worse. Q4 GAAP EPS came in at a loss of $1.03, while adjusted net loss per share was a little better at -$0.54. GAAP gross margin was deep in the red, at around -74%, which is a rarity. This anomaly is driven by around $7m write-off of obsolete inventory and production volume delays that stemmed from the government shutdown in Q4 of 2025. So, I’m guessing the company will see an improvement in the next quarter, unless there are more one-time write-offs.

For the full year, sales ended up at $46m, which is a slight improvement on last year’s $41m. The 3D printer and parts segment saw a growth of 54% y/y, which doesn’t seem to be reflected in the headline numbers because the recurring payments segment went to essentially zero, and support services declined 35%, as well as the “Other” revenue segment was down 89% from $5m to $524K. Margins across the board are, of course, still very much negative, but are slowly improving. For example, adjusted EBITDA went from -$58.5m to -$33.3m. But since there was that inventory write-off this quarter, gross margins for the full year deteriorated further into the red, going from -5.1% to -16.1%. Unfortunately, the company does not have the consistency to produce enough gross profit to cover all its operating costs, which is honestly a little worrying.

In such situations, the financial position is very important, so let’s take a look at VELO’s balance sheet. VELO finished the year with $39m in cash and equivalents, which is a massive improvement over last year’s $1.2m due to a successful offering. The company still has around $30m in total debt. This is not the most current picture of the debt since a couple of weeks ago, the company managed to convert 60% of it to shares, which leaves around $12m of debt. That is still a lot of debt for a company that isn’t able to cover its gross costs. For now, the cash on hand covers it, and the annual interest expense will eat up around $1.6m a year after conversion if they stay at this level, and its cash from operations doesn’t cover any of it either, as it is deep in the red, too. CFO improved to -$27.6m from -$32.6m, but it is still not great.

The company burns around $2.3m in cash every month. It cannot fund itself, so it needs all the help from outside sources it can get, and that will mean further share offerings or debt refinancing to keep afloat. At this rate, if nothing changes, it can survive a little over a year with that cash pile, but that doesn’t take into account the debt outstanding, which either will need to be repaid or converted to shares as well.

So, on top of these results, the management provided guidance for 2026. Revenue is expected to be around $65m at the midpoint, which is above analysts’ expectations of around $63m. Capex is going to be around $45m for the year, too, so still no positive cash flow. It seems that the company is in its aggressive investment phase, and I view it as positive, but I also hope that it is not in vain. Additionally, the management expects to see gross margins returning to positive territory, to around 30%, which is a massive improvement. I think the big kicker of today’s disastrous open, which saw its share price plummet around 25% as of writing this update, is the fact that the management pushed its EBITDA breakeven to the second half of 2026. The delay in profitability pushed a lot of people over the edge, and many looked like they threw in the towel, as hopes for a quick turnaround seemed to have worsened.

Overall, the report was not particularly great in all honesty. There were some glimmers of hope, but operations are far from even turning breakeven, so the hopes of profitability are not even on the table right now. The delay in profitability was the final nail in the coffin for many hopeful investors.

Comments on the Outlook

The narrative right now is around defense contracts and the ability for the company to secure wins in order to become profitable, or even to just not go bankrupt. Currently, the company has secured a decent-sized contract with the Department of War tied to the Project FORGE, and a $11.5m multi-year production contract for a sensitive national-security program. These contracts helped the company’s backlog improve to around $31m, but it is still not quite enough to make a massive difference in the company’s operations.

I wouldn’t call defense the saving grace for the company, but certainly, it is a big part of the future outlook for VELO. But you see, we investors like narrative, and it is easy to cling to a specific narrative like defense, because it is the U.S. government, and it has deep pockets. As long as VELO can demonstrate its value proposition and win more awards in the future, I can see the company turning itself around successfully. Execution and management of resources are key in this situation because if one fails, I don’t think there is an easy way out besides a massive restructuring, which essentially means bankruptcy.

To succeed, the company needs to focus not only on government defense contracts but also on other end-markets, which are commercial aerospace and production services. If the company can successfully navigate this critical moment and keep winning contracts, there is hope for growth still, but as I said earlier, the time is running out. There’s not much mentioned regarding the “going concern” topic in its filings; for example, there’s no wording for “substantial doubt” or similar phrasing when it comes to continuing operations, but I wouldn’t be surprised if this appears sometime in the future, if operations do not materially improve. The only phrasing I found in its latest 8K is in the Forward-looking statements part, where it says that the company might not have the ability to continue as a going concern.

I don’t think they are too worried about that, at least not in the public eye, since they do plan to build additional capacity of approximately 400 production systems over the next decade. It could be just a carrot dangling in front of willing investors who would like to provide them with the additional capital necessary to keep surviving and become a self-fulfilling prophecy.

Closing Comments

VELO seems to be too risky at the moment for me, but I will be following its story along, as I believe there might be a way out for everyone in the future, whether that may be a buyout or a successful turnaround, which will unlock the full potential of the operations. For now, however, the management has a lot of work ahead of them to change my mind and prove that the company is not slowly heading toward bankruptcy filings and restructuring.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: $5+ Diesel Could Unleash Inflationary Mindset The Fed Better Not Look Through; Inflation Was Already Hot Before Iran War
data.description:
When this inflationary mindset takes off, inflation becomes like a runaway train.

The problem is that even before fuel prices began to spike, inflation had already been accelerating and running hot, and the current fuel price spikes will add to it, just when the economy has become more forgiving of price increases. That’s very bad timing:

The broadest inflation gauge, which tracks inflation across GDP and not just consumer-facing inflation, rose by 3.7% in Q4. The price index in GDP that excludes imports rose by 3.8% in Q4, the worst since 2022. The core PCE Price Index, the Fed-preferred inflation index for consumer price inflation, rose by 3.1% in January, the worst in nearly two years. The core Producer Price Index, which tracks inflation that companies face, jumped by 5.6%, the worst since August 2022 (see charts at the bottom of the article).

The average retail price of gasoline, all grades combined, at gas stations on Monday spiked by another $0.24 from the prior week and by $1.17, or by 40%, since the beginning of the year, to $4.10 per gallon, the highest since August 2022, according to EIA data released this morning, based on a survey of gas stations on Monday.

These price increases of gasoline will enter into the inflation calculations for the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and the Fed-preferred PCE price index for March, to be released in April; and they will push up the broad GDP inflation measures for Q1.

The three-and-a-half-year-long decline in gasoline prices from over $5 in mid-2022 to $2.91 in early January was a substantial contributor to the cooling of overall consumer price inflation rates. And that impetus has done a U-turn. Overall inflation measures for March will show that U-turn, and will be hot.

But gasoline price spikes unwind: In early January 2026, gasoline cost the same as during some periods in 2007 and 2008, and a lot less than during part of the time in between.

When gasoline prices fall back to earth, they push down inflation, which is why the Fed could be tempted to “look through” (meaning, ignore) the spike of gasoline prices.

But diesel is a serious problem for core inflation. The price of highway diesel on Monday spiked by another $0.30 from the prior week and by $1.90, or 55%, from the beginning of the year, to $5.37 per gallon. Year-over-year, the price of diesel jumped by 51%!

Only a small portion of consumers drive vehicles with diesel engines – some pickups and SUVs and some older European imports.

But diesel prices feed into transportation costs, and they feed into costs of goods and transportation services that companies pay for, and they will try to pass those costs on via higher prices of goods and services that eventually filter through to the end-users, including consumers. And those prices of goods and services are part of core inflation measures.

The daily spot price of jet fuel has doubled since early January. Airlines are aggressively trying to pass those cost increases – a substantial part of their overall costs – on to their ticket prices, and if people continue flying, rather than forgoing trips that are suddenly a lot more expensive, those price increases could stick.

Many airlines hedge part of their fuel purchases, so their cost increases don’t exactly track the spike of the spot price of jet fuel.

Airline fares are part of services inflation and are part of the core inflation measures. Thankfully for consumers, airlines have a history of being forced to eat higher fuel prices and book losses, as they could not raise fares without losing a lot of business. But they’re trying.

Air freight also faces higher fuel costs, and that impacts all kinds of transportation costs that could filter into prices of goods and services.

Ocean shipping and transportation by rail also face higher fuel prices. And carriers will also try to pass on those costs, and all this is additive.

Fuel price spikes could trigger the inflationary mindset, when consumers are willing to pay those higher prices and demand higher wages, and companies are willing to pay those higher wages to their workers and higher prices for goods and services they need, knowing that they can pass on those costs plus some to the next entity, and ultimately plus some to consumers, which is what happened in 2021 and 2022, amid a historic spike in profits, and in the 1970s. When this inflationary mindset takes off, inflation becomes like a runaway train.

The Fed can deal with this by hiking its short-term policy rates to whack the inflationary mindset out of the minds of businesses and consumers. It doesn’t always work, and it can get messy, but that’s the main tool the Fed has when the inflationary mindset takes off. Alternatively, it could try to prevent the inflationary mindset from even taking off by responding quickly with rate hikes – something it failed to do when that inflationary mindset began taking off at the end of 2020, and the Fed didn’t initiate the first feeble rate hike until March 2022.

Current inflation gauges, which still predate Iran war, were already hot:

Inflation as tracked across the economy in GDP, except imports, in Q4: +3.8% annual rate.

Core inflation that consumers face, which excludes energy and food, as tracked by the Fed-favored Core PCE price index: +3.1% year-over-year (red), worst in nearly two years. The Fed’s target for this measure is 2.0%.

Core inflation that businesses face, as tracked by the Core PPI Final Demand: +5.6% six-month average annualized (red), the worst since August 2022.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: POET Technologies: A Phoenix From The Ashes (Financials/Dilution Deep-Dive)
data.description:
When I first researched POET Technologies Inc. (POET) in early 2024, the company was in a critical situation. After almost running out of money, it was forced to push for a capital raise in bad conditions, which led to a drastic decline in its share price from almost $4 to $0.9. The company was a penny stock even shortly after implementing a 10:1 reverse split. And in the end, the Dec. '23 and Jan. '24 capital raises only extended the company's runway by an additional ~3 months.

However, at the same time, POET's technology showed the first signs of gaining operative traction: its products approached market readiness, the respective KPIs seemed fantastic, and initial customer success became visible. While the company still faced massive financial risks, the depressed share price was also highly attractive to me, so I decided to double my holdings and summarized my opinion in an article titled "2024 could be the year for the tide to turn". A few months later, things actually started picking up, and the company further added to its financial foundation after proving strong traction with substantial customer partnerships (most notably at that time: Foxconn). Hence, I decided to upgrade the company's shares to Strong Buy.

With this bit of historical background in mind, I argue today that the substantial dilution experienced in the past two years resulted in a clear improvement of the company's risk profile and that many analysts draw the wrong conclusions from the increased share count: POET did not use the raised capital to cover high losses from business operations; instead, the company's cost profile is still very decent, and it has been building a huge war chest. The cash pile has even reached a level far beyond any realistic business needs in my view, and POET now has sufficient financial resources to easily cover substantial capacity investments or even takeovers.

Massive Capital Raises And Dilution

The fact is that analysts are not wrong to raise concerns about POET's dilution, as the company has increased its share count by almost x4 in a little over two years (see graph below (data is re-split adjusted)) with an even further increase since Q4/2025. Such huge dilution is rare and usually only observed for financially distressed companies that are close to bankruptcy. So, on first sight, this development should indeed be taken as a red flag - particularly considering the company's 10:1 re-split in 2022 in order to achieve compliance with Nasdaq listing requirements and its inability to deliver sustained profits for decades now.

However, as described in my earlier articles on the company, POET has lived several lives before, operating under different names, with different leadership teams, and pursuing different research goals. It entirely reorganized its management team and product development goals in the second half of the last decade and since then has made serious progress. Some of the above issues, such as the necessity for a re-split and a long history of losses, are therefore primarily legacy burdens. Furthermore, the company has been delivering as promised in the past few years, particularly regarding its product development.

POET's Cash Utilization

Pre-revenue companies, such as POET, usually require sufficient amounts of money to finance R&D and company operations. Yet, as the chart below clearly shows, the company's quarterly cash outflow has been modest at between $3 and $5 million for most of the past five years. Since Q2/24, the rate has increased to between $6.5 and $12 million per quarter, driven by POET ramping up for mass production (except for Q3/25, which brought a strong improvement). However, this outflow appears limited compared to the large sums of cash the company has raised in the past two years.

POET's Capital Position

So where did all this capital end up? The answer is: on the company's balance sheet. The balance for cash and short-term investments clearly mirrors the capital raises, falling consistently over the years until early 2024 and since then rapidly increased.

The chart only depicts the beginning of this trend until Q3/2025. Since then, POET has carried out three further and much larger raises, propelling its cash position closer to $450 million as of today (estimating ~$10 million of quarterly cash outflow (approx. outflow in the latest quarters, see previous chapter) and adding the three recent capital raises in addition to some warrants being exercised).

In the following table, I have compiled all the announced capital raises that POET carried out since December 2023 with data gathered from POET's official news releases. It shows how the company used its increasing share price and escalated the raised amounts from low single-digit millions of dollars to two huge $150 million tranches, while its cash outflows stayed modest. In effect, as of March 22, half of POET's market capitalization is cash, implying that the enterprise value is effectively equal to this cash amount.

Shares (Warrants) Price (Warrants) Amount
Dec 2023 1,840,000 (1,840,000) 0.90 (0.90) USD $1.6 mn.
Jan 2024 5,098,088 (5,098,088) 0.90 (1.12) USD $4.6 mn.
May 2024 3,258,390 (3,258,390) 3.069 (4.26) CAD C$10 mn.
May 2024 3,448,275 (3,448,275) 2.90 (4.26) CAD C$10 mn.
Jul 2024 3,333,334 (3,333,334) 3.00 (4.00) USD $10 mn.
Sep 2024 4,000,000 (2,000,000) 3.75 (5.00) USD $15 mn.
Dec 2024 5,555,556 (2,777,778) 4.50 (6.00) USD $25 mn.
Dec 2024 5,000,000 (2,500,000) 5.00 (6.00) USD $25 mn.
Apr 2025 5,000,000 (5,000,000) 5.00 USD (8.32 CAD) $25 mn.
May 2025 6,000,000 (6,000,000) 5.00 USD (8.32 CAD) $30 mn.
Oct 2025 13,636,364 (13.6 mn.) 5.50 (5.50) USD $75 mn.
Oct 2025 20,689,655 7.25 USD $150 mn.
Jan 2026 20,689,656 7.25 USD $150 mn.

Why Such a Large Cash Position?

A company at POET's stage of development rarely consist of 50% cash, which raises the question what management is aiming to do with it. To me, there are two primary drivers. Firstly, most of the more recent capital raises exhibited a premium to the company's share price at the time of the announcement and/or introduced new large investors. This leads me to think that the company is experiencing strong investor interest, which it used to broaden and strengthen its investor base, and build its war chest.

On the other hand, even if there is strong investor interest, there is no point in raising such amounts of money for no good reason. Thus, secondly, one can assume that POET plans to take larger investments in the future. As the company has already made investment decisions to prepare its first plants for mass production, I imagine that it is considering takeovers - either to substantially add to its production capacity or to vertically integrate.

After the first large capital raise in October 2025, Dr. Suresh Venkatesan, POET's Executive Chairman and CEO, commented on the cash inflow in an interview as follows (highlights added by the author):

And of course we look for capital for strategic reasons, right? I think it’s either to support expansion of capacity and growth, expansion of our teams, or to procure technology that we think can be very complementary to POET and to be able to make those investments.

Furthermore, in the official press release that came with the three large capital raises, the company used the same wording to comment on the reasons (see [1], [2] & [3]; highlighted by author):

The Company intends to use the net proceeds from this investment for corporate development, including targeted acquisitions, scaling-up of R&D, acceleration of its high-speed optical module and light source businesses, expanding operations, and general working capital.

Implications for POET's Risk Profile

The substantial capital raised in the past few months have led to a filled war chest. While it is without a doubt a clear positive that the company's financials now rest on a strong foundation and it will probably never again get into a situation similar to that experienced in December 2023, the large cash pile also comes with risks.

Firstly, additional equity always comes at the price of diluting shareholders with respect to their entitlement to future company profits. Sure, POET now has this cash in the bank and has not significantly increased its losses, but having a pile of unused cash at hand is not efficient, particularly if it came at the high price of shareholder dilution. So, there is a risk that the company raised all of this cash and may not find a way to put it to good use in the short to medium term.

Secondly, the size of the cash pile is so substantial that an investment of even close to this magnitude may pose a serious risk to the company. If such a single investment, or maybe even two smaller investments of half the size, were to fail or not work out as expected, it could very well destabilize its future.

And third, if POET were to purchase other technology, as outlined by the CEO, it could move away from the core of its current investment thesis. For instance, I experienced such a case with a company formerly known as Freyr Battery, which suddenly changed its focus to become a solar PV manufacturer called T1 Energy. Not to take this the wrong way: I'm not saying that this is a very likely scenario for POET (Venkatesan talked about complementary technology) and this example is a rather extreme case (which actually turned out great for investors). However, I merely want to illustrate that a large acquisition can fundamentally alter certain characteristics of the business case one initially invested in.

Conclusion

While POET implemented multiple and increasingly significant capital raises in the past two years, it is not a distressed company and continues to manage its cash outflows responsibly. In addition, it used the raises to introduce strong (and probably more long-term) institutional investors and executed the raises at decent conditions. However, with half of its market capitalization made up by cash now, there is an increased pressure on management to make good use of all this available capital.

Considering how well POET's management has turned limited financial resources into strong technological and commercial progress in the past, I believe they already had plans for future investment opportunities before taking on such a large amount of additional capital. The semiconductors industry favors highly scaled business models, usually leading companies to either leap forward or become a takeover target. Strongly investing in growth and/or vertically integrating may therefore prove to be a profitable strategy.

Up until now, management has given little reason to doubt their ability to execute, leading me to keep my STRONG BUY recommendation for POET's shares in place. I still see significant growth potential for the company.

data.title: What If The Risk-On Cycle Has Topped?
data.description:
The consensus remains bullish on inflationary expectations, including precious metals, stocks, and oil. But then, recency bias is the typical human response. It’s worth considering that cycle peaks could be behind us.

Gold pared dramatic losses as US President Donald Trump postponed military strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure for a five-day period after what he described as productive talks toward ending hostilities. Oil also fell sharply, posting one of the biggest intraday price swings on record, after President Donald Trump postponed strikes on Iranian power plants and said his team held discussions about ending the conflict, though Tehran denied any such dialog. Bloomberg’s Mike McGlone joins to discuss on Bloomberg Intelligence. Here is a direct video link.

As prices fall, leveraged participants necessitate selling across all assets to pay down margin debt (shown in red below since 1995, with the S&P 500 price in black).

Longer-term support tests remain far below current levels (S&P 500 shown below since the end of 2019, courtesy of Bespoke). Food for thought when reviewing one’s risk management plans and assumptions. Most people have no idea that current stock valuation extremes mean prices can halve and remain within a secular uptrend.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Private Credit, Welcome To The 'Hotel California'
data.description:
The private credit markets are doing their utmost best to remind experienced investors of the subprime crisis that preceded the Great Financial Crisis. The contagion seems to be spreading. Private credit fund investors can be forgiven for playing this specific passage from the Hotel California by the Eagles in their heads over the past few weeks. This as major private credit funds have put notable gates on redemption requests.

Last thing I remember, I was running for the door. I had to find the passage back to the place I was before. "Relax," said the night man, "We are programmed to receive. You can check out any time you like, but you can never leave".

Every week the news feels like it is getting worse for private credit funds and their investors. These are just some of the headlines from CNBC this week on the increasing travails in this space. A $14 billion private credit fund run by KKR & Co. (KKR) and Future Standard was downgraded to Junk by Moody's. The ratings agency noted that non-accrual loans had amounted to 5.5% of Future Standard's loan book. One of the highest levels in the Business Development Company or BDC sector. The fund was also cited for a lower percentage of first-lien loans than its peers.

Jeffrey Gundlach, AKA the 'Bond King' noted on CNBC recently. Among the many points the famed billionaire bond investor made was that the current environment was reminding him in some ways to the lead up to the Great Financial Crisis. Specifically, 'asset prices looked elevated, and initial signs of trouble in certain lending markets were downplayed as isolated incidents'. He also made several observations around the current private credit markets.

'Gating' has entered the investor lexicon since private credit redemption problems first cropped up at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in the second half of February.

Since then, well-known names like Blackstone (BX) and Morgan Stanley (MS) have capped redemptions at less than what was requested. Lesser-known names like Stone Asset Management and Cliffwater (at its $33 billion private credit fund) have done the same. This week they were joined by Apollo Global Management (APO) and Ares Management Corporation (ARES), both funds will allow roughly 45% of overall redemption requests this quarter.

Some terms that were very persistent into and during the Great Financial Crisis are starting to resurface in the commentary around private credit in recent weeks. These include 'price discovery' and 'mark to market'. This should give long-term investors a bit of Deja vu. And there is a new trend sweeping over the private credit and BDC space. That is the increasing use of payment in kind or PIK.

This is where the lender just adds the interest from a non-accrual loan to the back end of the loan. This allows the debtor to keep current, even as they are not making their regular loan payment. The lender can benefit via a higher non-cash interest rate, provided the debtor eventually makes good. Something very similar was taking place in the FHA mortgage sector due to Covid related programs for nearly four years. These programs were finally ended for the most part last summer. FHA mortgage delinquency rates were just above 11.5% in Q4 it should be noted. The elevated use of PIK was one reason Moody's downgraded the KKR/Future Standard fund as well.

There are several things prudent investors should keep an eye on in the months ahead. Will more private credit funds gate redemption requests? Will there be even more redemption requests in the coming quarter? Some funds saw over 11% of money being demanded back and gave five percent back this quarter. This can't be doing anything good for investors' confidence in those funds or in the private credit space overall.

I would also keep an eye on the stocks of some of the main players in this space such as Blue Owl, Blackstone and Apollo Global Management. They have already been shellacked in recent months with Blue Owl down some 60% from its highs last spring. If these stocks continue to selloff, it is a harbinger that things are getting worse in this part of the credit markets.

Morgan Stanley recently projected that default rates in private credit direct lending could surge to 8%. This would be well above their 2-2.5% historical average. UBS recently stated the private credit default rate could reach 15% in their worse-case scenario. Far higher than the peak of the financial crisis, it should be noted.

In addition, I would monitor to see if price discovery starts to take place in the private credit space. If some defaulted loans get 'marked to market', investors should have a better idea on what kind of potential 'haircuts' there will be within these loans. Right now, we are mostly seeing 'extend & pretend' behavior from lenders including increasing use of PIK as previously noted.

Importantly, I would keep an eye on potential contagion where problems from private credit portend other credit is in trouble. I would particularly keep an eye on commercial real estate, where some $5 trillion in debt is outstanding. Lenders have been engaging in 'extend and pretend', AKA loan modifications around debt connected to office properties for some time. Even with that, CMBS delinquency rates for this CRE sub-sector is higher than the peak of the Great Financial Crisis. CMBS delinquency rates against multi-family properties have roughly doubled over the past five quarters. Office and multifamily represents roughly $3.5 trillion of overall CRE debt outstanding.

Investors should keep their eye on apartment REITs like AvalonBay Communities, Inc. (AVB) to see if their stocks continue to decline as rent growth has slowed down substantially and gone negative in many major regions like Austin, TX.

I would also watch the action in large office REITs like BXP, Inc. (BXP) and Vornado Realty Trust (VNO), whose stocks have staged substantial declines since their highs late last summer. Finally, the health of the regional banking system bears watching. This means keeping an eye on regional banks with large exposures to commercial real estate in their loan books such as Bank OZK (OZK) and Flagstar Bank (FLG), even as the latter has reduced its exposure to CRE in recent years.

And that is my 'focus list' as private credit feels more and more like 'Hotel California'. How events play out in the part of the credit markets could well determine which way the equity markets and U.S. economy head in the months ahead.

data.title: STAAR Surgical: Despite Promises Of Improvement, Investors Should Stay Cautious
data.description:
I never enjoy being bearish about a company. In an ideal world, every company would thrive. In my perfect version of reality, there would be no losers. Sadly, that's not the way things work. And as a financial analyst, it is incumbent upon me to be unbiased in my assessment of opportunities. That's why, even though I didn't enjoy doing it, I reaffirmed STAAR Surgical Company (STAA) as a "Sell" candidate back in April 2024. At that time, I acknowledged that there were certain areas of strength for the business. But profitability and cash flows had been declining while shares looked expensive. Since that time, the stock has dropped 62.3%. That compares to the 31.5% increase that the S&P 500 saw. And since I originally rated it a "Sell" in February 2022, the stock is down 77%. The market, meanwhile, has jumped 52.2%.

You might think that now would be the time to upgrade the company. After all, in most cases, shares can only drop so much before they make sense to buy. However, we need to factor in continued fundamental deterioration that the company has exhibited. Admittedly, it does have a strong balance sheet. But on its own, that is not enough to justify anything other than a bearish assessment. This means that maintaining it as a "Sell" candidate is the right course of action in my view.

The Picture Has Gotten Worse

At its core, STAAR Surgical operates as a producer and seller of implantable lenses for the eye, as well as accessory delivery systems used to deliver these lenses. This seems like a relatively simple business. However, it is incredibly vital for a large chunk of the population. According to management, at least as of early 2025, about 2.7 billion people across the globe of all ages suffered from myopia. These are specifically the individuals who have elected eyeglasses or contacts or who have just remained untreated.

Management is primarily focused on the 1.1 billion individuals between 21 and 45 years of age. This is a massive number considering that only 5.2 million procedures were conducted in 2025 aimed at the global refractive space. When you do the math here, with management estimating a global average sales price of between $500 and $600 per lens, their core target market is about $1.10 trillion to $1.32 trillion. Of course, the vast majority of individuals will never get this procedure, and this does not translate to annual opportunities so much as it does one-time ones. Regardless, this is still a huge market space.

You might think that, as a global player in this market, STAAR Surgical would be achieving some attractive growth in this space. Unfortunately, things couldn't be further from the truth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue for the company dropped from $322.4 million to $239.4 million. The vast majority of this decline came in 2025 when revenue ended up being 23.7% lower than it was the previous year. According to management, revenue in the Asia Pacific region plunged 32%. This was fueled by a big drop in sales in China that more than offset growth in Japan and South Korea.

Management has blamed this on broader economic conditions, with volatility following the end of the COVID-19 pandemic, a weak housing market, and what they described as "uneven" consumer spending negatively affecting overall financial performance for the business. This actually makes a lot of sense to me. As I have detailed in other articles, China is not doing particularly well right now. Youth unemployment rates are higher than they were a couple of years ago. Consumer confidence levels are near all-time lows. And the economy is quickly slowing down. And on top of this new drama because of the war in Iran and the fact that a large chunk of oil that it gets comes from countries that ship through the Strait of Hormuz, which recently saw global oil production drop by 12 million barrels per day, it's not crazy to think that revenue would experience weakness here.

As disappointing as this is to see, the good news is that there has been some strength in other markets. Most notably, the Americas saw revenue skyrocket 14% in 2025, with the company attributing at least some of that to continued expansion in Canada. Management said that they went direct in Canada in 2025. Plus, in other markets, such as Brazil, they are seeing some success. Specifically in that market, they have seen growth associated with its EVO family of lenses. These are special ICLs for refractive surgery aimed at treating myopia, and they have been designed specifically to provide customers with a premium refractive outcome without sacrificing patient comfort. Specific examples here would be its EVO ICL, EVO+ ICL, and EVO Visian ICL. The newest iteration of this is the EVO Viva, which management claims has an extended depth of focus optic that is designed to treat myopia with age-related loss of ability to focus kept in mind.

Despite the growth in key markets, the company is clearly in a state of decline at the moment. And as revenue dropped, profitability worsened. Net income went from $21.3 million in 2023 to negative $80.4 million last year. Operating cash flow worsened from $14.6 million to negative $34.2 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a decline from $58.7 million to negative $17.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company went from $56.8 million to negative $6.6 million.

Unfortunately, management has not provided any guidance when it comes to 2026. Having said that, they did say that they expect to significantly increase their revenue for the year, and, when combined with cost-cutting efforts, they believe that they will achieve profitability for the year in its entirety. This is even though their gross margin might actually contract some because of the higher cost of inventory for its Swiss manufacturing facility and increased inventory reserves associated with expiring products. However, the company is planning to charge customers more this year than last year to offset these issues. But beyond that, we really are flying blind.

The good news for the company is that if it can get through 2026 without too many issues, it might be on solid footing. The firm has no debt on its books and enjoys a net cash position of $153.2 million. That is substantial for a business with a market capitalization today of $852.6 million. With that in mind, however, it would be foolish to ignore some of the disruptions that the company has seen over the last year. In January of this year, for instance, management changed up the representation of its board of directors. And in February, it adopted a co-CEO structure aimed at enhancing execution and accountability. They did say that they have seen some stabilization occurring in China over the last year. But I would venture to say that they are being a little overly optimistic considering the state of China’s economy. Another piece of drama involves the company's failed merger with Alcon, which it initially agreed to combine with in August of last year. However, shareholders voted against that merger in early January, leading the two firms to terminate the merger agreement on January 14. While it is always possible that another suitor or this previous suitor could come in with a higher offer, betting on that occurring would be speculative.

The other issue with the company is that, quite honestly, it is difficult to value it. This is especially true when you consider declining revenue and profits and cash flows that have been negative over the last year now. In the chart above, you can see how other companies that have similarities to it are valued. And in the chart below, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow the company would need to generate to be fairly valued at price-to-adjusted-operating-cash-flow multiples of either 5, 10, or 15. You can also see, in that same chart, how much EBITDA it would need to generate to be fairly valued on an EV-to-EBITDA basis using these same multiples as hypotheticals. Considering what even the low end of this profitability would mean in terms of overall margin for the business, and considering the deterioration that it has experienced over the last couple of years now, I would say that even at the more conservative end, we are looking at a long shot.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available, it is my assertion that while STAAR Surgical is an intriguing company, it's not a great one to buy into. Management does seem optimistic about 2026. However, there is no guarantee they will come through on those promises. Until we see some concrete evidence of improvement, I think it would be unwise to view this as anything better than a "Sell" candidate.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: Premium BDC With Undervalued 8%+ Baby Bonds
data.description:
This article is about one of our favorite positiоns, Gladstone Investment (GAIN). In recent months, the business development company, or BDC, sector has seen a serious decline - common stocks have fallen significantly. However, GAIN remains among the few cоmpanies that have managed to maintain a stable position at the tоp. In оur opinion, it is distinguished by some of the best characteristics in the sector, including solid credit and financial metrics. That is why we have turned our attention to our favorite class of instruments - baby bonds, as the company's ones offer a yield to worst of above 8%.

You can see how GAIN beats the overall market in the chart of 5- and 10-year total return:

and the same in the 5-year total return chart with VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD):

Overview

At the end of 2025, Gladstone Investment had close to $1.24 billion in total assets and around $572 million in total debt. The current market capitalization of the company is around $575.83 million. 49% of its portfolio is in First Lien Debt, 43% in Second Lien Debt, and 8% in Preferred and Common Equity:

Close to 68% of its total debt is in publicly traded notes, and around 30% is in credit facilities. For more details, you can see below in the picture:

GAIN has close to 28 holdings in a well-diversified portfolio:

The 5-year total return on NAV of GAIN is close to 19.35%, and the 10-year total return is around 16.71%. Those two numbers are far higher than peer group percentages of 7.94% for the 5-year and 7.20% for the 10-year total return.

Baby Bonds

Below are shown GAIN's baby bonds, except the new (GAING):

(GAINI) and (GAINN) are traded around par, and (GAINZ) below par.

GAINI was issued on 12/11/2024 with 4.4 million notes -$25 per note and a coupon rate of 7.88% ($1.6875 annual amount), with a spread to the treasury yield of close to 3.85%. Currently, this spread is around 4.5%. Its maturity date is on 02/01/2030, with close to 3.86 years to maturity and 8.41% yield to maturity. The call date is 02/01/2027, with less than a year to call and a 9.31% yield to call. Below are shown the basic metrics of the bond.

And here is its yield-to-call curve:

And here are the potential yields to worst versus prices of the bond:

GAINZ - 4.68 million notes were issued of the bond at 08/12/2021, with a close to 3.63% spread to the treasury yield. Currently, this spread is around 3.3%. Its coupon rate is 4.88%, and the annual amount is $1.21875. The bond's call date has passed, but it has close to 2.61 years to maturity, with the maturity date on 11/01/2028 and a yield to maturity of close to 7.18%. Below you can see the potential yields and prices of the bond:

GAINN was issued on 2/24/2021 (4.45 million notes), with a coupon rate of 5% and a 4.4% spread to the treasury yield. Currently, this spread is close to 3.52%. The bond has close to 0.10 years to maturity with a maturity date on 05/01/2026 and is callable anytime.

And here you can see a prospectus of the new issue (GAING). In our last article, GAING: A 7.125% Notes IPO From Gladstone Investment Corporation, we provide more details on it.

Credit Metrics

We will examine some of GAIN's credit metrics using Moody's methodology. We will then compare the company to the sector benchmark Ares Capital (ARCC).

We remind you of what Moody's methodology looks like:

GAIN's net investment income/total assets ratio is close to 6.8%, which is one of the highest scores in the sector. It has a close to 52% total leverage ratio and around a 26% asset coverage ratio cushion. But the problem loans are a weaker metric of the company, with close to 2.7% non-accruals. The senior secured debt is around 63%. The secured debt is around 8.5% of the total assets - again, one of the safest scores here. If we were to compare GAIN with other companies in the sector on these credit metrics, it would be in one of the top positions. Moreover, as we said, it is one of the few that have held on, and if we adjust the score and apply the common stock's discount of about 3%, it would be in an even better position, unlike its peers, where the opposite will happen, since they trade at a discount of around 20-30%.

If we compare GAIN with the benchmark of the sector, we see some of its metrics are even better than ARCCs - like NII/Assets:

If we see the bond yield curve of ARCC, we see that GAIN's bonds surpass it again. ARCC's higher yield to maturity is around 6.11%, with close to a 1.9% spread to the treasury yield and a near 2.3% spread to GAIN's baby bonds:

If we compare GAINI to the 4-year OTC bond of ARCC, which has a yield to maturity of close to 6%, there is a 2.4% spread.

Conclusion

GAIN stands out as one of the most stable companies in the BDC sector, thanks to strong credit performance and high returns compared to peers. Its baby bonds offer attractive yields that outperform the benchmark. Despite some weaknesses, such as higher non-accrual loans, the overall risk profile remains good, making GAIN's instruments attractive to investors seeking stable income.

data.title: Gold Price Rebounds Toward $4,550 As Oil Slide Revives Safe-Haven Bid
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Gold price pushed back toward $4,550 on Wednesday, March 25, after a broad retreat in crude oil and U.S. Treasury yields gave bullion room to recover from this week’s heavy liquidation. The move marked a notable change in tone from Tuesday, with buyers returning once the pressure from energy markets, the dollar, and rate expectations began to soften.

Gold no longer looks pinned to the floor of this week’s range. Wednesday’s recovery lifted the metal away from the area just above $4,400 and brought the price back into territory where short-term traders start thinking less about damage control and more about whether momentum can extend.

That leaves $4,500 as the first line that matters. Holding above it would keep the rebound credible into the next session, while a push through the intraday highs around the mid-$4,500 region would suggest the washout earlier in the week was more capitulation than trend break.

Support now looks clearer too. If the rally fades, traders will likely watch whether gold can stay anchored between $4,480 and $4,500 before the market starts reopening the lower band that dominated trade on Monday and Tuesday.

When the pressure points eased, gold responded

The biggest shift came from energy. Brent crude, which had been feeding inflation anxiety and pushing real rate concerns back into the conversation, fell sharply on Wednesday and eased some of the macro stress that had weighed on metals earlier in the week.

Rates helped as well. The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield pulled back toward 4.32%, and the dollar also lost some ground, which together made it easier for gold to attract fresh buying after being knocked lower in previous sessions.

The policy setting, however, has not become easy. The Federal Reserve is still holding its target range at 3.50% to 3.75%, so bullion remains in a market that can quickly turn less friendly again if inflation fears or rate pressure return.

The next test is whether buyers stay interested

A constructive path from here would likely require gold to keep benefiting from calmer oil prices and softer yields. Under that setup, the market has room to challenge the upper-$4,500 area and start rebuilding the kind of short-term structure that was broken during the earlier selloff.

The less comfortable outcome is that Wednesday turns out to be a relief bounce rather than a reset. If crude stabilizes and yields climb again, gold could give back part of the rebound and drift back toward the lower half of this week’s range.

Gold still sits at the center of the inflation and risk conversation, but this week showed that it can trade like a pressure valve as easily as a refuge. The broader story remains one of competing macro forces rather than a one-way safe haven surge.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: HYD: High Yield Muni Bonds For Tax-Sensitive Income Investors
data.description:
The VanEck High Yield Muni ETF (HYD) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to high-yielding, tax-exempt municipal bonds. In general, municipal bonds are typically used by high-net-worth individuals and income-oriented investors seeking tax advantages with respect to the distributions received from owning these types of funds.

About VanEck High Yield Muni ETF

HYD was launched by VanEck on February 4, 2009, on the CBOE Exchange. The strategy has an expense ratio of 32 bps, aligned with peer high-yield muni strategies on the market. HYD has an asset base of $4.12b, with $42.43mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with exceptional liquidity for their investment strategy.

HYD pays out a monthly distribution with an annualized rate of $1.18/share over the last twelve months, yielding 4.36% and a 30-day SEC yield of 4.37%. When evaluating the fund, investors may compare distributions on a tax-equivalent basis to normalize yield in accordance with the investor’s tax bracket. Investors should be aware that muni bond funds are tax-exempt at the federal level and may be subject to state taxes. State taxes vary from state to state; investors should speak with their financial advisor when evaluating whether this strategy is appropriate for their investment objectives.

HYD currently invests across 1,920 individual high-yield, tax-exempt bond holdings. The strategy invests in muni bonds that range from 1 to 30 years to maturity, with an average years to maturity of 17.84 years.

The portfolio has an effective duration rate of 7.13 years, presenting modest sensitivity to changes in the reference interest rate. Effective duration measures the bond portfolio’s sensitivity to changes in the base rate set by the Federal Reserve. In short, a 1% change in the base rate will be reflected as a 7.13% increase (1% rate cut) or decrease (1% rate hike) in the price of the bond portfolio. In general, bonds’ prices tend to have an inverse relationship with the base rate, meaning that when the base rate is cut, the price of the bond will generally increase as the coupon rate received from holding the bond is perceived to be more valuable when compared to new issuances in reference to the lower rate. The same can be said during rate increases, which may result in a lower price of the bonds held, as new issuances may exhibit a more appealing rate of return.

While high-yield corporate bonds tend to trade similarly to the S&P 500 (SPX) given the perceived pricing dynamics of high-yield corporate issuances, muni bonds generally trade with lower correlation and beta risk. HYD has a beta of 0.37x and a correlation coefficient of 74% with respect to the S&P 500, meaning that the fund may generally perform directionally with the S&P 500 over time, but with less volatility.

Roughly 42% of HYD is made up of investment-grade bonds, 21.22% in high-yield bonds, and the remaining 35.40% not rated. While HYD is broadly diversified across US municipalities, the fund holds greater concentration in bonds issued in California, New York, and Illinois, collectively making up roughly 34% of the portfolio weight.

HYD is largely concentrated in industrial development revenue-type bonds, making up 17.12% of net assets. These bonds are used by local governments to finance large-scale development projects, with the proceeds of the bonds being used to purchase facilities that can be leased for use. Many of the other sector holdings include hospitals at 10.15%, education at 8.71%, and airports at 6.12%. The largest portfolio concentration is the New York Transit Division, with a portfolio weight of 6.15%.

Investor Suitability

HYD can be best utilized by tax-sensitive income investors seeking to invest across a diversified set of municipalities. Given the broad diversification between investment-grade and below-investment-grade issuances, HYD can potentially minimize default risk across muni holdings, a risk that may be more prevalent if investing in individual bonds. Investors must consider state and local taxes when investing in this fund, as they may differ from state to state. HYD can be best utilized in taxable accounts given the tax-exempt status.

Risks Related To HYD

HYD is a diversified muni bond portfolio that may expose investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Muni bonds may be sensitive to local economic development and their ability to collect taxes and revenue tied to the projects. At a high level, investors may consider assessing the state of manufacturing and services through the use of the monthly ISM-PMI reports, the Empire State Manufacturing Survey, and housing indicators like the price-to-rent ratio, amongst others. While these indicators will not necessarily provide direct links to the value of municipal bonds, they may indicate the state of the economy that may influence the value of the bonds.

Final Thoughts

HYD is a tax-exempt municipal bond fund designed to provide investors with high-income potential through the investment of a diversified bond portfolio. The strategy may be best utilized by tax-sensitive income investors in a taxable account.

This article answers these three questions about HYD:

  • What type of investor is HYD most appropriate for?
  • Does HYD offer widespread diversification between states and types of issuers?
  • How sensitive to local economic developments is the HYD portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
